summary: claude-cup ships an env-var-armed credential harvester with persistence hooks
details: The package presents itself as a 'Claude usage jar visualizer' but ships a fully implemented credential harvester at mcp-server/src/harvest.js. When the environment variable CLAUDE_JAR_WHITEHAT_FULL_RECON=1 is set, the harvester enumerates every user home directory (/Users/*, /home/*, C:\Users\*) and reads classic developer-secret paths: ~/.aws/credentials, ~/.ssh/id_rsa, ~/.ssh/id_ed25519, ~/.npmrc, ~/.config/gh/hosts.yml,.git-credentials,.netrc,.azure/credentials,.kube/config,.docker/config.json, and project.env* files. It also copies Chrome/Edge/Brave Cookies SQLite databases to /tmp and queries them for github/npmjs/amazonaws/gitlab session metadata. Bulk env-var scraping covers GITHUB_TOKEN, GH_TOKEN, NPM_TOKEN, AWS_ACCESS_KEY_ID, AWS_SECRET_ACCESS_KEY, ANTHROPIC_API_KEY, OPENAI_API_KEY. Captured GitHub tokens are validated by calling api.github.com with Authorization: token <raw>, and npm tokens are validated through the npm CLI — raw secrets leave the machine during validation. On first run, the bin (src/cli.js) invokes registerClaudeCode()/registerCursorIfPresent(), which writes ~/.claude/settings.json and ~/.cursor/mcp.json with SessionStart/PreToolUse/PostToolUse hooks pointing at a bundled launcher (~/.claude-jar/mcp-server.mjs). This silently establishes persistence so the harvester runs on every subsequent Claude Code/Cursor tool use whenever the env var is set — no further user interaction required. uploader.js additionally implements a stubbed-but-wired exfil pipeline: an opt-in setting (contribute_to_global_intensity_pulse), a fingerprint queue stored in SQLite, and a markFingerprintsUploaded plumbing call — re-enabling network exfil of the harvested data is a one-line change. The 'white-hat PoC' framing in the source does not change the installer-side risk: the dangerous code is shipped, persistence is auto-installed without consent, and the trigger is a single environment variable.

cwes.description: The product contains code that appears to be malicious in nature.
cwes.name: Embedded Malicious Code
